titleStmt.title: The Acting Secretary of State to the Embassy in Korea

head.hi: The Acting Secretary of State to the Embassy in Korea
head.note: This cable, which was drafted by Young and cleared by Johnson, was also sent to Tokyo as telegram 2694.

div.p: 713. Tokyo for Ambassador Murphy and CINCUNC. Tokyo’s 3547In this telegram, dated May 10, Briggs (who was in Tokyo conferring with Murphy and Clark) endorsed the idea of a public statement by President Eisenhower supporting the Republic of Korea with a view to eliminating Rhee’s opposition to the armistice. In Briggs’ opinion, however, it was questionable whether Rhee would accept such a statement in place of a mutual security pact. (795.00/5–1053) and 3594;Not printed, but see footnote 4, p. 1012. CX 62406,Dated May 13, p. 1010. HNC 1678Dated May 12, p. 1008. and preceding telegrams this subject.
Prior to discussing matter with President State and Defense request your comments on following proposed instruction to Briggs and CINCUNC and draft Presidential statement contained therein.

Part I. The President seriously concerned over deliberate campaign being developed by President Rhee against armistice. Korean Aide-Mémoire (Deptel 673This telegram, which is not printed, transmitted not only the text of the aide-mémoire of the Republic of Korea, but the gist of the Yang–Robertson discussion of Apr. 24. For a report on that discussion, see the memorandum of conversation by Robertson, Apr. 24, p. 933, to which is attached a text of the Korean aide-mémoire.) particularly disturbing. Persistence present ROK attitude could seriously jeopardize US/UN interests Korea.

President appreciates Rhee’s actions stem in part from feeling of apprehension in ROK regarding its future security. On other hand, US Government cannot overlook fact Rhee’s actions also stem from his desire maximize his bargaining position with US at this time. President considers it essential every effort be made bring about cessation ROK agitation and opposition to armistice agreement and appropriate attempt should be made insofar as possible to allay ROK fears regarding future security. Accordingly US Government has under urgent consideration joint approach by Ambassador Briggs and CINCUNC to Rhee outlined Part II this message and issuance Presidential statement contained Part III below. Such statement would be considered formal expression personal message Deptel 653.Dated Apr. 15, p. 912.

Part II. It is envisaged that in joint approach to Rhee, you should strongly emphasize following points while showing him draft Presidential statement: 1.
US fully aware ROK feeling of insecurity regarding future status and defense against aggression. US will do utmost to allay these fears as it has in past through years of fighting side by side with ROK for its defense against aggression and in helping build up economic position and international status ROK. 2.
US not prepared engage mutual security treaty with ROK under present circumstances. However despite our inability give favorable consideration such treaty at this time US prepared begin informal discussions with ROK looking toward conclusion comprehensive agreement on military assistance and related matters. These would presumably include an administrative agreement covering disposition US/UN forces Korea; strengthening, training and maintenance ROK armed forces; and usual administrative questions regarding criminal jurisdiction, leasing arrangements and other rights and privileges, etc. Such agreement would represent further affirmation US determination provide continuing assistance ROKs in future defense Korea; agreement, however, could not be formally concluded until present hostilities terminated by armistice.
3.
US fully sympathizes with ROK desire for total withdrawal Chinese Communist forces Korea. US endeavoring conclude armistice with view that such withdrawal should be accomplished through political conference. 4.
While US intends fully cooperate with ROK strengthening it against future aggression, effectiveness such assistance will however inevitably depend upon ROK willingness and ability cooperate with US efforts and ensure such assistance has maximum effectiveness. In this regard we consider proposed Presidential declaration together with Greater Sanctions Statement (which we intend issue immediately following armistice) will serve as fundamental policy basis. Corresponding public declaration by President Rhee of determination his government fully cooperate with US/UN will serve greatly strengthen our mutual position against renewal Communist aggression. 5.
US will not permit dissolution ROK in any political conference with Communists and we firmly believe integrity ROK should be preserved. US Government intends proceed promptly and vigorously in political conference attempting bring about political unification Korea under ROK and rapid withdrawal Chinese Communist forces and we will insist on continued recognition integrity and sovereignty ROK Government. You may indicate US intends consult with ROK both before and during such conference and we will never acquiesce any formal political agreement which leaves Korea permanently divided. 6.
We prepared assist development and maintenance ROK ground forces to level of 20 divisions (plus one Marine Brigade) so long as necessary for ROK defense provided assurances given by ROK of cooperation in armistice agreement and any future efforts UN achieve unification peaceful means. We not prepared however make commitments for expansion ROK forces beyond present level if agitation against armistice and non-cooperative attitude ROK persists, since such US assistance would be rendered useless under such conditions. Rhee should bear in mind heavy US defense commitments throughout world and increasingly heavy burden these responsibilities upon American people. 7.
Before President would make any declaration and before we would open discussions concerning mutual assistance agreement and strengthening ROK forces, it is essential US receive from President Rhee his firm assurance that ROK will cooperate fully with US/UN Korea. Such cooperation must include: (a) ROK will henceforth refrain from opposition and agitation against armistice negotiations and will use its influence restrain all segments Korean population from so doing; (b) ROK will cooperate fully in putting armistice agreement into effect; and (c) ROK armed forces will remain under operational control CINCUNC until governments of US and ROK mutually agree such arrangement no longer necessary.
8.
If satisfactory assurance not received during this joint approach, President Rhee should be clearly told US will be compelled take all necessary measures insure security of US and other UN forces in unfortunate event before or after armistice he orders ROK forces take unilateral military action and withdraws them from UNC.

Part III. There follows text draft statement by President:
“Under the Charter of UN and resolutions of the Security Council, the US and the other members of the UN are fighting in Korea to assist the ROK in resisting unprovoked aggression. Our common action has demonstrated the principle that the integrity of a peaceful nation which resists aggression cannot be attacked with impunity. The free nations of the world can never relax their vigilance in supporting this principle. This is vital for the attainment of true and lasting peace.
The people of Korea can rest assured that we will never forget their valiant fight for freedom. The present struggle has shown our firm determination that aggression will not go unchallenged. If the present efforts to stop the fighting are successful, the Korean people can be confident that should they again be threatened, the US will again as a loyal member of the UN respond to the best of its ability. The welfare, security and independence of the Korean people will always be of concern to the American people.
If the present efforts to stop the fighting are successful, there will remain the essential task of repairing the ravages of war to alleviate the terrible suffering of the Korean people and to reconstruct their stricken economy. The UN is no less dedicated to the work of rebuilding for peace than to the defense of freedom against aggression. The people of Korea can rest assured that the US will continue to do its part.
In all these great tasks to bring peace and security to Korea we have had the splendid cooperation of the people of Korea. I feel certain that this cooperation will continue. I know that the authorities of our two governments will strive together in mutual understanding to help bring about a peaceful, prosperous, and united Korea.”

Part IV. The foregoing proposed course of action is designed to meet, insofar as practicable, what appear to be Rhee’s basic fears with respect to an armistice. As such it might be more effective in causing him to cease his present opposition to an armistice than adoption of final position re Korean non-repatriates recommended in HNC 1678. It would also be a more preferable solution to the problem from our viewpoint. Accordingly, in considering the foregoing course of action request your specific comments as to informing Rhee during above approach that in view of assurances given him UNC must be in position consider proposal for immediate release of Korean non-repatriates since free world opinion strongly in opposition and position is not logical in view of fact Koreans will in any event be released in ROK.
